front: What window lists every GameObject in the open scene in a parent-child tree?
back: The Hierarchy window — it shows all GameObjects in the active scene. Selecting one highlights it in the Scene view and loads its properties in the Inspector.

front: What is the Inspector window used for?
back: Viewing and editing the properties and components of the currently selected GameObject or asset. Every component (Transform, Rigidbody, scripts) appears as a panel here.

front: What keyboard shortcut focuses the Scene view camera on a selected object?
back: Press F (or double-click the object in the Hierarchy) to frame-select — the Scene camera zooms and centers on the selected object.

front: What are the keyboard shortcuts for Move, Rotate, Scale, and Rect tools?
back: W = Move · E = Rotate · R = Scale · T = Rect · Q = Hand/Pan · Y = Universal (all transforms)

front: What is the difference between the Scene view and the Game view?
back: Scene view is the editor viewport for building and navigating the level. Game view shows exactly what the player camera sees — it activates when you press Play.

front: What is a Prefab?
back: A reusable asset that stores a configured GameObject (with all components and child objects). Instances placed in scenes share the prefab; edits to the prefab propagate to all instances automatically.

front: What does the Project window show?
back: A file browser of all assets in the project's Assets/ folder — scripts, textures, audio, prefabs, scenes, and materials. Assets only exist in Unity once placed here.

front: What does the Console window display?
back: Log messages (Debug.Log), warnings, and errors from scripts and Unity's engine, both in Edit mode and during Play mode. Essential for debugging.

front: In what order are Awake, Start, and Update called?
back: 1. Awake() — called once when the script loads (even if disabled). 2. Start() — called once before the first Update, only if the script is enabled. 3. Update() — called every frame.

front: What is FixedUpdate() and when should you use it?
back: Runs at a fixed physics timestep (default 0.02 s, 50×/sec) regardless of frame rate. Use it for all physics-related code: applying forces, reading Rigidbody velocity, etc. Never use it for input polling.

front: What is LateUpdate() and when is it useful?
back: Called every frame after all Update() calls finish. Best used for camera follow logic — so the camera reacts to final player positions rather than mid-frame positions.

front: How do you get a reference to a component on the same GameObject?
back: GetComponent<T>() — e.g. GetComponent<Rigidbody>(). Returns null if no matching component exists. Cache the result in Awake to avoid repeated lookups.

front: How do you create a copy of a prefab at runtime?
back: Instantiate(prefab, position, rotation) — returns the new GameObject instance. Quaternion.identity means no rotation. Optionally pass a parent Transform as a fourth argument.

front: How do you destroy a GameObject? Can you add a delay?
back: Destroy(gameObject) removes it at end of the current frame. Destroy(gameObject, 2.5f) delays destruction by 2.5 seconds. For components: Destroy(GetComponent<T>()).

front: What does Time.deltaTime represent and why multiply by it?
back: The time in seconds since the last frame. Multiplying movement by it makes speed frame-rate independent — a 60 FPS game and a 30 FPS game travel the same distance per second.

front: What attribute exposes a private field in the Unity Inspector?
back: [SerializeField] — e.g. [SerializeField] private float speed = 5f; Keeps the field private in code but editable in the Inspector. Prefer this over making fields public.

front: What is a Coroutine in Unity?
back: A method that can pause execution mid-way (using yield return) and resume in a future frame or after a delay — without blocking the main thread. Declared with return type IEnumerator.

front: How do you start and stop a Coroutine?
back: Start: StartCoroutine(MyMethod()) or StartCoroutine("MyMethod"). Stop by name: StopCoroutine("MyMethod"). Stop all: StopAllCoroutines().

front: What is the difference between OnCollisionEnter and OnTriggerEnter?
back: OnCollisionEnter — physical contact between two regular colliders; objects push each other. OnTriggerEnter — one collider has Is Trigger = true; objects pass through, only overlap is detected.

front: What is Input.GetAxis() vs Input.GetKeyDown()?
back: GetAxis("Horizontal") returns a float from -1 to 1 (smooth, analog). GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Space) returns true only on the frame the key is pressed (digital, one-shot).

front: What does [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody))] do?
back: Forces Unity to automatically add the specified component when this script is added, and prevents removal of that component while this script exists. Avoids null reference errors.

front: What component adds physics simulation to a 3D GameObject?
back: Rigidbody — enables Unity's physics engine to control the object with gravity, velocity, and forces. Without it, the object is static and won't respond to physics.

front: What is the key difference between a Trigger and a regular Collider?
back: A regular Collider blocks objects and produces a physical collision response (bouncing, sliding). A Trigger (Is Trigger = true) detects overlaps but lets objects pass through freely.

front: How do you apply a force to a Rigidbody?
back: rb.AddForce(direction * magnitude) — optionally specify ForceMode: Force (continuous), Impulse (instant, mass-aware), VelocityChange (instant, ignores mass).

front: What ForceMode applies an instant velocity change that respects mass?
back: ForceMode.Impulse — applies force / mass as an instant velocity delta. For the same impulse, lighter objects accelerate more. Use for jumps, explosions, knockback.

front: What does Physics.Raycast() do?
back: Casts an invisible ray from origin in direction; returns true if it hits a collider and fills a RaycastHit struct with data (point, normal, distance, GameObject reference).

front: What is the Layer Collision Matrix?
back: Found in Project Settings → Physics. A grid defining which layers can collide with which others — useful for ignoring collisions between allies, bullets, and triggers.

front: What component gives character movement without a Rigidbody?
back: CharacterController — provides collision-based movement with built-in slope limiting and step offset handling. Uses controller.Move() instead of forces. Good for deterministic player controllers.

front: How do you prevent a Rigidbody from spinning due to physics?
back: Use Rigidbody Constraints → Freeze Rotation on the unwanted axes. For a 2D top-down character, freeze all rotation axes. Alternatively, set Angular Drag very high.

front: What component plays animations on a 3D GameObject?
back: The Animator component — it references an Animator Controller asset and drives animation playback, blending, and state machine logic.

front: What is an Animator Controller?
back: A state machine asset (created in Project window) that defines animation states, the transitions between them, and the parameters (bool, float, int, trigger) that drive those transitions.

front: How do you trigger a transition from a C# script?
back: Call animator.SetTrigger("Name"), animator.SetBool("Name", true), or animator.SetFloat("Name", value) on the Animator component. The Controller reacts to parameter changes automatically.

front: What is a Blend Tree?
back: An Animator state that smoothly blends between multiple animation clips based on one or two float parameters — e.g., blending idle/walk/run based on a Speed float.

front: What is an Animation Clip?
back: A recorded timeline of keyframe data describing how GameObject properties (position, rotation, material color, etc.) change over time. The source material for Animator states.

front: What is an Animation Event?
back: A marker placed on an Animation Clip timeline that calls a function on a script attached to the same GameObject at a specific frame. Used to sync SFX, VFX, or gameplay logic to animation.

front: What is a Unity Avatar in the context of Mecanim?
back: A skeleton mapping definition for a humanoid or generic rig. Humanoid avatars let Unity retarget animations between different character models that share the same bone hierarchy.

front: What is the Mecanim animation system?
back: Unity's modern animation system featuring the Animator Controller state machine, Blend Trees, humanoid Avatar retargeting, IK solving, and Animation Events. Replaced the legacy Animation component.

front: What three components make up Unity's core audio system?
back: AudioClip (holds the audio data) + AudioSource (plays the clip) + AudioListener (hears the audio — one per scene, typically on the Main Camera).

front: How do you play a sound at a world position without a persistent AudioSource?
back: AudioSource.PlayClipAtPoint(clip, position) — a static utility method that creates a temporary AudioSource at the world position, plays the clip, and auto-destroys when done.

front: What does 'Play On Awake' do on an AudioSource?
back: Automatically starts playing the assigned AudioClip when the scene loads or the GameObject becomes active. Enabled by default — disable it if you want to trigger playback from a script.

front: What does Spatial Blend = 1 mean on an AudioSource?
back: Fully 3D audio — volume attenuates with distance from the AudioListener, and panning changes based on direction. 0 = fully 2D (flat, no position effect). Values in between mix both.

front: What is an Audio Mixer?
back: An asset that routes AudioSources into groups (Master, SFX, Music) for collective volume control and DSP effect chains (reverb, compression, EQ). Audio Mixer snapshots can be triggered at runtime.

front: What is the difference between AudioSource.Play() and PlayOneShot()?
back: Play() interrupts any currently playing audio on that source. PlayOneShot(clip) layers the clip on top of whatever is playing, ideal for rapid SFX that shouldn't cut each other off.

front: What is the AudioClip Load Type 'Streaming'?
back: The clip streams from disk while playing instead of loading entirely into memory — best for long music tracks. 'Decompress on Load' is best for short SFX; 'Compressed in Memory' is a middle ground.

front: What are the four built-in light types in Unity?
back: Directional (sun — infinite, directional) · Point (bulb — all directions from a point, limited range) · Spot (cone shape) · Area (rectangle — bake-only in Built-in RP).

front: How does a Directional Light differ from a Point Light?
back: Directional — uniform direction across the entire scene, position doesn't matter, infinite range. Simulates the sun. Point — radiates from a single point in all directions with a limited range.

front: What is a Material in Unity?
back: A data asset that pairs a Shader (the rendering algorithm) with property values — textures, colors, metallic/smoothness — defining how a surface looks. Applied to MeshRenderer components.

front: What does 'baking' a light mean?
back: Pre-computing lighting (including indirect bounces/GI) and storing results in Lightmap textures on static geometry. Baked lighting is GPU-efficient but cannot change at runtime — it's for static environments.

front: What is Global Illumination (GI)?
back: Simulation of indirect lighting — light that bounces off surfaces and illuminates nearby objects. Can be Baked (pre-computed into lightmaps) or Realtime (dynamic, using Enlighten or LPPV).

front: What is a Skybox?
back: A cube or sphere material that renders as the scene's background environment (sky, space, city horizon). Assigned in Window → Rendering → Lighting → Environment. Also contributes to ambient lighting.

front: What does the Standard Shader support?
back: PBR (Physically-Based Rendering) with metallic/roughness workflow. Produces realistic surfaces that respond consistently to all light types. The default shader in Unity's Built-in Render Pipeline.

front: What is the difference between Realtime and Baked light modes?
back: Realtime — updates every frame, supports moving objects and dynamic shadows, more expensive. Baked — pre-computed into lightmaps, no runtime cost, but geometry and light can't move.

front: What is the Canvas component and why is it required for UI?
back: The root container for all Unity UI elements — Text, Button, Image, etc. must be children of a Canvas. It manages rendering mode, event handling, and screen scaling for all its UI children.

front: What are the three Canvas Render Modes?
back: Screen Space - Overlay (always on top, no camera). Screen Space - Camera (placed at a distance in front of a camera). World Space (3D canvas in the scene, can be seen from any angle).

front: What is an Anchor in Unity UI?
back: Four normalized points on the parent RectTransform that define how a UI element scales and repositions as the screen resizes. Well-set anchors are the key to resolution-independent UI.

front: What is the EventSystem and why is it required?
back: A required GameObject that processes input events for Unity UI (pointer clicks, touch, keyboard navigation). No EventSystem = no UI interaction. Unity creates one automatically with the first Canvas.

front: Why is TextMeshPro preferred over the legacy Text component?
back: TMP provides higher quality text rendering (signed distance field), richer styling (outline, shadow, gradients), better performance, and support for dynamic font atlases. It's Unity's officially recommended text solution.

front: How do you change a UI Image's sprite from a script?
back: Get a reference to the Image component, then set imageComponent.sprite = newSprite; The sprite must be a Sprite asset (Texture Type: Sprite in the Inspector).

front: What is a Canvas Scaler and what does it do?
back: Controls how the UI scales across different screen resolutions. Scale With Screen Size mode lets you set a reference resolution and scale the whole UI to match — the most common setting.

front: What does Raycast Target on a graphic component control?
back: Whether that graphic (Image, Text) blocks pointer events. Uncheck it on decorative graphics that shouldn't intercept clicks — prevents invisible hits on transparent overlapping images.

front: What is a Sprite and how is it imported?
back: A 2D graphic asset — an imported texture set to Texture Type: Sprite (2D and UI) in the Inspector. Can be sliced into individual sprites using the Sprite Editor.

front: What is a Sprite Atlas and why is it used?
back: An asset that packs multiple sprites into one texture, reducing draw calls — instead of one GPU draw call per sprite, all sprites from the atlas render in a single batch.

front: What does Sorting Order on a Sprite Renderer control?
back: The draw order within a Sorting Layer — higher numbers render in front. Sorting Layer is evaluated first; within the same layer, Sorting Order determines front-to-back order.

front: What is the Tilemap system?
back: A Unity 2D system for painting tile-based levels on a grid using Tile assets and the Tile Palette. Works with Tilemap Renderer, Tilemap Collider 2D, and Rule Tiles.

front: What is a Composite Collider 2D?
back: Merges multiple 2D colliders (e.g., all individual Tilemap Collider 2D tiles) into a single optimized physics shape, dramatically reducing physics calculation overhead for large tilemaps.

front: What is the difference between a PolygonCollider2D and a BoxCollider2D?
back: BoxCollider2D is a simple axis-aligned rectangle. PolygonCollider2D traces the exact outline of a complex shape — more accurate but computationally more expensive.

front: What does the Rigidbody2D Gravity Scale property do?
back: Multiplies the global gravity applied to this body. A value of 0 makes the object weightless (space games, floating platforms). Negative values make objects float upward.

front: What is the Package Manager?
back: A Unity window (Window → Package Manager) for installing, updating, enabling, and removing Unity packages — including the Unity Registry, your Asset Store purchases, and local/git packages.

front: What is Build Settings used for?
back: File → Build Settings configures the target platform (PC, WebGL, Android, etc.), the scenes to include in the build, and platform-specific options before exporting the final executable.

front: What does the Unity Profiler do?
back: Records per-frame CPU, GPU, memory, audio, rendering, and physics data to identify performance bottlenecks. Open via Window → Analysis → Profiler. Essential for optimization work.

front: Why must .meta files be committed to version control?
back: .meta files store Unity's import settings and the GUID (unique identifier) for each asset. Without them, Unity breaks all references between assets on other machines or after recloning.

front: What is PlayerPrefs used for, and what are its limitations?
back: A simple key-value store persisting data between sessions — suitable for settings (volume, resolution). Not suitable for save game data: limited types (float, int, string), no encryption, easily edited by players.
